items.title: Classification of surgical wounds
items.contentHtml:
Classification of surgical wounds

  • Class I (clean): without contamination, without entry into the respiratory/digestive/genitourinary tract; infection rate <2%.
  • Class II (clean-contaminated): controlled entry into the respiratory/digestive/genitourinary tract; rate <10%.
  • Class III (contaminated): significant effusion, acute non-purulent inflammation or recent traumatic wound; rate 15-20%.
  • Class IV (dirty/infected): pus, visceral perforation, traumatic wound with devitalized tissue; rate >30%.
  • Trap: a wound with pus is already Class IV, not Class III.

items.title: Preoperative risk factors
items.contentHtml:
Preoperative risk factors

  • Age >65 years: immunosenescence and comorbidities.
  • Diabetes mellitus: Preoperative HbA1c >7% doubles the risk of surgical site infection (SSI).
  • Obesity (BMI >30): tissue hypoperfusion and greater tension in wound.
  • Active smoking: vasoconstriction and hypoxia; suspend ≥4 weeks before.
  • Hypoalbuminemia (<3.5 g/dL): marker of malnutrition and worse healing.
  • Immunosuppression (corticosteroids, chemotherapy): increases the risk of opportunistic infection.

items.title: Adequate antibiotic prophylaxis
items.contentHtml:
Adequate antibiotic prophylaxis

  • Indicated in Class II, III and IV wounds and in Class I if there is a prosthetic implant, cardiac surgery, neurosurgery or immunosuppression.
  • Choice: Cefazolin 2 g IV (1 g if weight <80 kg) for most surgeries; in those allergic to beta-lactams: clindamycin 600 mg or vancomycin 15 mg/kg.
  • In colorectal surgery: cefazolin + metronidazole 500 mg or ertapenem 1 g.
  • Duration: single preoperative dose; Do not prolong more than 24 hours (except cardiac surgery with bypass, up to 48 hours).

items.title: Optimal timing of prophylaxis
items.contentHtml:
Optimal timing of prophylaxis

  • Administer within 60 minutes prior to incision (120 min for vancomycin and fluoroquinolones by slow infusion).
  • The serum peak should coincide with the time of incision; ideal: 30-45 min before.
  • If the surgery exceeds 2 half-lives of the antibiotic (e.g. cefazolin every 4 hours), repeat the intraoperative dose.
  • Trap: administering prophylaxis >2 hours before the incision is associated with a higher rate of SSI (equivalent to not giving prophylaxis).
  • In cesarean section: giving antibiotic 30-60 min before incision, not after cord clamping.

items.title: Superficial infection criteria
items.contentHtml:
Superficial infection criteria

  • Affects only skin and subcutaneous cellular tissue, within 30 postoperative days.
  • Must meet at least one of the following: purulent drainage from the incision; positive fluid or tissue culture; signs of inflammation (pain, edema, erythema, heat) and the surgeon opens the wound intentionally (except if culture is negative).
  • Does not include suture abscess or cellulitis without pus.
  • Trap: if there is superficial and deep infection in the same wound, it is classified as deep infection.

items.title: Deep infection criteria
items.contentHtml:
Criteria for deep infection

  • Affects fascia and muscle, within 30 days (or up to 1 year if there is an implant) postoperatively.
  • Criteria: purulent drainage from the fascia; spontaneous dehiscence or surgical opening of the wound with a positive culture; deep abscess; fever or local pain plus imaging findings (CT: collection with edge enhancement).
  • Requires surgical drainage and targeted antibiotics.
  • Trap: if the infection involves fascia, it is deep although it appears superficial; not to be confused with infected seroma.

items.title: Organ or space infection
items.contentHtml:
Organ or space infection

  • Affects any structure deep to the fascia (organ, cavity, space) that was opened or manipulated during surgery.
  • Examples: mediastinitis poststernotomy, intra-abdominal abscess postcolectomy, empyema postthoracotomy.
  • Criteria: purulent drainage from drainage placed in the organ/space; positive fluid/tissue culture from site; abscess or other evidence of infection on imaging (CT/MRI) or reintervention.
  • Trap: an organ/space infection does not require that the superficial wound be infected; There may be fever without skin signs.

items.foco: Staphylococcus aureus as a common pathogen
items.title: Staphylococcus aureus as a common pathogen
items.contentHtml:
Staphylococcus aureus as a common pathogen

  • Staphylococcus aureus is the most common agent in SSI (20-30%), followed by E. coli and Enterococcus.
  • In clean surgery S predominates. aureus (nasal colonization is an independent risk factor).
  • MRSA (methicillin-resistant Staphylococcus aureus): consider in patients with previous hospitalization, hemodialysis, known carriers or surgery with prosthetic implant; use vancomycin or daptomycin.
  • The production of biofilm on implants makes eradication difficult; requires removal of the device.
  • Cat: do not use first generation cephalosporins if MRSA is suspected.

items.foco: Use of drains and their management
items.title: Use of drains and their management
items.contentHtml:
Use of drains and their management

  • Suction-closed drains (Jackson-Pratt, Blake) reduce fluid accumulation, but do not prevent SSIs; they can be a route of entry for infection if not managed aseptically.
  • Indications: surgery with dead space, suboptimal hemostasis, expected anastomotic leak.
  • Remove as soon as possible: in <24-48 hours if discharge <30 mL/day and without leak; do not maintain >7 days unless specifically indicated.
  • Trap: open drains (Penrose) increase the risk of ascending infection; prefer closed systems.
  • Do not use prophylactic antibiotics only due to the presence of drainage; The indication is surgery, not drainage.

items.title: Management with wound opening
items.contentHtml:
Management with wound opening

  • If superficial SSI is suspected, open the wound (remove stitches/staples) for drainage and debridement if there is pus or necrotic tissue.
  • Take deep tissue culture (not just superficial swab) and send for aerobes/anaerobes.
  • Start empirical antibiotic therapy directed at skin flora and the surgical site; adjust according to antibiogram.
  • Open wound: management with moist healing (dressings with saline solution, alginates or hydrocolloids) and closure by secondary intention or delayed closure when the bed is clean.
  • Trap: not primarily closing an infected wound; Wait until there are no signs of infection and a negative culture.

items.title: Culture and directed antibiogram
items.contentHtml:
Culture and directed antibiogram

  • Sample collection: deep tissue or pus aspiration (avoid superficial swab due to contamination with commensal flora).
  • Send for aerobic and anaerobic culture; if mycobacteria or fungi are suspected, request specifically.
  • Antibiogram: allows de-escalation empirical therapy; if you isolateS. aureus sensitive to methicillin, use cefazolin instead of vancomycin.
  • In polymicrobial infections (abdominal surgery), cover gram-negative and anaerobes; adjust according to local resistance.
  • Trap: negative culture does not rule out infection if the patient received previous antibiotics; repeat if signs persist.

items.title: Intraoperative prevention measures
items.contentHtml:
Intraoperative prevention measures

  • Skin preparation: antisepsis with 2% alcoholic chlorhexidine (superior to povidone iodine in reducing SSI).
  • Normothermy: maintain body temperature ≥36°C (hypothermia causes vasoconstriction and hypoxia tissue).
  • Oxygenation: FiO2 at 80% during surgery and 2 hours postoperatively (in patients with normal lung function).
  • Glycemic control: maintain glucose <180 mg/dL intraoperative and immediate postoperative.
  • Aseptic technique: double glove, sterile fields, limit traffic in operating room.
  • Trap: shaving with a razor increases microabrasions; use electric clipper or do not shave.
